title: The pass closes on the last carter
The carter brings the factor and a sealed letter
The settlement settles in for the dark
The factor calls on the ironmaster to audit
Night of the first deep snow
The herbwife is called to the master's house
The widow finds him at the ledger desk
Morning: the ironmaster is dead
Coll is handed the charter and the cellar key
The body is laid in the cold chapel
The chaplain says the words; no one says murder
The factor produces the company's sealed letter
The clerk cannot make the weights add up
The collier counts the charcoal sheds
The furnace-keeper warns the bloom is going cold
The soldier keeps to the smithy and watches
The two prospectors ask after the assay records
The child says the master had a visitor that night
The cellar is opened; the stores are short
FORK: open the ledger, or ration by need
Coll breaks the seal on the master's accounts
The last ledger page is in a different hand
The clerk reads the falsified weights aloud
The ledger names the collier as the debtor
The collier denies the debt before the village
The factor claims the books for the company
The widow withholds what she saw
The herbwife is asked about the medicine
A prospector recognises the factor from the lowlands
Coll rations the short cellar against the ledger
The cook reports meal spoiling in the damp
The smith mends the furnace tools on credit
Midwinter: the furnace bloom fails
The bonded servant falls sick with the fever
FORK: the factor's company plan, or the village's
Coll backs the factor's company plan
The factor takes the cellar key and the stores
Rations cut to the company's lowland scale
The collier withdraws his hearths from the work
The fever spreads in the cold houses
The herbwife runs short of the heart-tincture
The herbwife tells Coll what the tincture was for
Coll learns the master was dying already
The widow breaks and tells what she saw at the desk
Coll matches the altered page to the master's own hand
The factor's letter is read in full at last
The thaw-wind comes early off the pass
FORK: lay it before the law, or settle at Grayloam
Coll resolves to lay it before the law
Coll writes the account for the coroner
The clerk seals the true and the false ledgers together
The factor argues the company will be ruined by it
The pass opens; riders come up for the company
Coll gives the coroner the whole winter's account
The collier is cleared; the factor's audit is exposed
ENDING (law): the truth goes down the pass on record
Coll resolves to settle it at Grayloam first
Coll calls the settlement to the chapel
Coll lays the dying man's guilt before them all
The collier's debt is struck from the books by vote
The widow agrees the death will be called illness
The factor is held to a settlement-made bargain
The pass opens; one account goes down, agreed by all
ENDING (settle): Grayloam keeps its own counsel
Coll backs the village's plan over the factor
The collier takes charge of the hearths and stores
The village pools rations by need, not by ledger
The factor is shut out of the cellar
The herbwife treats the fever with the village's stores
The herbwife tells the collier of the master's illness
The collier guesses the ledger was the master's own lie
The soldier confronts the factor over the audit letter
The prospectors offer to carry word down for the village
The widow confides in the herbwife, not in Coll
The thaw comes; the furnace is relit by the village
The collier faces the company riders at the pass
The factor leaves with the company, the audit unfinished
ENDING (village): the settlement holds the charter itself
Coll refuses the ledger and rations by need
The collier is given the keeping of the stores
The sealed accounts are left unopened in the chest
The factor protests that the charter is being defied
Rations by need carry the village past the worst
The furnace falters but the hearths are kept warm
The bonded servant recovers under the herbwife's care
The child says again the master had a visitor
Coll questions the factor about the audit night
The factor admits he confronted the master that night
The herbwife tells Coll of the heart-tincture and illness
Coll pieces the death together without the ledger
The widow confirms the master took his own medicine
The thaw comes with the accounts still sealed
The company riders demand the unopened accounts
Coll hands down the sealed chest and his own plain word
ENDING (ration): the books open in the lowlands, not here
